oneLine: Urban autonomous passenger miles scale in steps; liability, insurance, and utilization adjust with a lag—skewing outcomes toward fleet autonomy enablers and away from slow-to-reprice personal-auto exposure.
coreThesis: Economically meaningful urban AV miles scale in steps; regulation, insurance, and OEM economics lag technology. That lag skews outcomes toward fleet-deployed autonomy enablers and compute-heavy validation, while legacy personal-auto pools and slow pivots absorb friction.

drivers.title: Urban AV miles and trip share
drivers.detail: Permitted robotaxi / ride-hail miles and share of trips in target metros—slope and persistence matter more than launch headlines.

drivers.title: Collision frequency and severity
drivers.detail: Comparable ADS vs. human baselines where data exist; carrier loss-cost commentary as a coarse check on pool repricing.

drivers.title: Regulatory path for fleets
drivers.detail: Domain expansion, incident response, and permit-to-service clarity—often the binding cap on scale.

drivers.title: Liability and indemnification
drivers.detail: How accident cost shifts as the human driver’s role in the stack shrinks.

drivers.title: Utilization and residuals
drivers.detail: Fleet economics and passenger-vehicle residuals as miles migrate toward on-demand supply.

winners: NVDA — AV compute, training, and simulation load tied to fleet iteration
MBLY — ADAS / map content scaling ahead of full driverless economics
losers: ALL (short) — personal-auto exposure if frequency narrative lags severity
F (long, broken) — legacy OEM pivot underperformed fleet-first and silicon-heavy legs
whyMatters: If the thesis is right, risk and returns reprice around miles, liability, and utilization—not a single autonomy headline. The dashboard ties positions to those observables and states what would falsify the view.
